transcript.transcript: Johnson runs from about 1850 to about 1920. That's in the series of pictures I've been keeping. This picture here of Andrew Southeast was the chief of the Coeur d'Alene Indians in 1870. And he's the one that made the treaty with the United States government that opened up the territory for settlement. He made his first treaty with Fred Post. It started Post Falls in 1870, which is the first time they ever had a treaty between an individual and a tribe of Indians. And Steve Liberty, who was the fur trader who lived here from 1860 to 1910 with the Coeur d'Alene Indians, read and interpreted the treaties with the Coeur d'Alene Indians. And He was so well liked by the Indians that they gave him the Lake, Liberty Lake, which has been named after him. And then some of his descendants still live here in Coeur d'Alene. And some of the other pictures that I had of Matthew Hayden, who was a fur trader also, who lived at Hayden Lake. And the lake was named after him. He was married to an Indian girl who was his daughter-in-law, who was a sister of Chief Gary, chief of the Spokane Indians. And their story is quite an interesting one, too. And then, of course, later on, the fort came in here. And that was, those soldiers were brought here by Colonel Merriman in 1878. And the fort was selected by General William Tecumseh Sherman in 1877. The men were assigned here from Georgia. And many of them stayed here after they were here. Some of them, like John Fernand, for whom Fernand Lake was named. And others, Best was another one, Frank Best, for whom Best Avenue and Best Mountain was named after. And then One of my old favorite friends was the Martin Meehan, the bugler at Fort Sherman. I knew him when I was a child. He used to like to tell us kids all about the days of the fort around here. Then later on, they took these boys out and brought in a detachment of Negroes. They had a little trouble with them, between the Indians and the Negroes. They couldn't get along, so they had to take the Negroes out of the area. That's when they had trouble with Kellogg and Wallace, too. The Negroes were the reason why Governor Steuenberg was assassinated, because the Kellogg and Wallace people didn't want to be shoved around by a Negro army. So they believed that the apathy caused by that was the reason that Governor Steuenberg was assassinated. Harry Orchard didn't. And some of his henchmen didn't care about it, so they put dynamite on the dated Governor Steuenberg's home and blew him up. And that was – and then, of course, later on, that was the old Kellogg Mining Stripe history, and then later on we had other local history too where people came in here and settled and homesteaded the Ross Point homestead. I have a picture of that and then I've got other homesteads up in French Gulch. That was named because everybody that lived up in the Gulch was a Frenchman, so they called it French Gulch. And then I have other pictures of hunting pictures. Some of our famous hunters, like Tommy Jimmy House and Tommy Hooper who were, who used to hunt cougar and game for the Cattlemen's Association, the Sheepherders' Association. And then we had the railroads that came in in 1886, which were usually which were laid here by the Coeur d'Alene Indians. The ties were made by the Coeur d'Alene Indians for the railroad under the supervision of Steve Liberty. And that, of course, is how, one of the reasons he was so well admired, because he led them into a lot of enterprises. What sort of enterprises did he make? Well, making railroad ties and raising wheat, farming, and then the grit mill at Post Falls. Fred Post hired the Indians to grind the wheat to make flour to sell to Spokane Falls, which eventually became a big industry, this grid mill. And then later on, they put a sawmill at Post Falls in 1871. There was two enterprises right there at Post Falls within a couple of years, from about 1871 to 1875. And I guess we have other things that we can show people that we have here, too. The fort itself, the way it looks then, the way it looked before the junior college was built down there. This is built down in the days. No, that's the one that's down there. Now it's a replica of that picture. That picture right there. See, over here is where the park is. And over here is where the baseball park is. You see that was right there where we'd be coming down, what is that, Garden Avenue? Or right there by the baseball park, that roof line in there. So that's actually where the entrance of the fort was. This would be the other side. See, this was the hospital back here. And this is where the prison was here. And these two guards standing here. Then you went in this way and around behind this place. And then back over here. And over this way would be where your church is. And then this would all be the junior college area now. So I guess that would be some of it. Here's a theater we used to have on Court Lane and Fourth Street. Was that live theater? Mm-hmm. They had a lot of shows there. They had Eddie Foy and his family of little boys. And that was there from 1904 to 1908, and then it burnt down. Had another one that was quite prominent there, this barber friend of mine. He used to be this lady's personal chauffeur and guide when she came here. And that was Evelyn Nesbitt, who was married to Thaw. And Thaw killed Stanford White in New York City because he thought he'd had an affair with her. And he shot him to death in a nightclub up there. And both these men were millionaires. And of course, everybody wanted to see what a woman looked like that two millionaires would kill themselves over. So they had her go on tour. And she came here to Coeur d'Alene. Her agent was getting a haircut from this barber friend of mine. And he'd been here from 1907 until about 1950 when he retired. But he used to tell me about this. That he was shaving this agent of hers. And he said something about, he said, we wish we had a young man who had a nice car that would take Miss Nesbitt around and show her the country while she was here. He said, well, he says, I'm single and I've got a nice car. And he says, well, then why don't you be her personal escort and take her around while she's here? He took the week off and they paid him a nice substantial fee to be her escort. He felt very privileged to think he was one of the gifted people to get to know her. And they made a movie of that incident. It was called The Girl in the Red Velvet Swing. And Ray Milan played the part of Stanford White, And Farley Granger played the part of Thaw. It's made a fantastic movie, too. Now, let's see. There's other stories I can tell you about that are all relevant to the history of this country. Here's a picture of Pappy Boynton's father. He was on a rowing team we had here. His father was a dentist in Coeur d'Alene here in the early days. That's another one. This man here is a bugler at Fort Sherman, Martin Meehan. I knew him, like I said before. He was here when he was about 16 years old, and he stayed here all of his life. He tells us. He said it was taken in 1890. It says Fort Sherman Garrison. Yeah, that's Fort Sherman Garrison. That's down there about where the junior college is now, about where the junior college would be located, about where Kildall's library is now. But anyway, Martin was telling us about one time he was on a milking detail. And they had their barns for the fort down there where Fernand Village is. And they'd milk the cows, and they were bringing their milk back to the fort in those big 10-gallon containers. And they saw these little fish swimming in that water that runs between Fernand Lake and Coeur d'Alene Lake. So they took their hats off and they spooned up a lot of these small fish and put them in the milk. And the boys had fish in their milk when they had that for breakfast the next morning. They weren't very happy with them over that, so they threw them in all the milking detail in the icy cold water to teach him a lesson. That's another little story I was told about. Now, is there anything else you can think of we need to elaborate on? Yeah, those are the fur trappers that I was telling you about that used to hunt for the Cattlemen's Association. So what sort of furs did they bring home? Those were lynx and cougars. Lynx and cougars, you know, right? They used to do it. Cougar Gulch was named because there was so many cougars in the gulch that they had to go up there and trap and wipe them out in order to make it livable for people to homestead up there. What's that? That's about 1935. That's the deer hunting party. My picture there. That's a picture up there with that old car in that 1902 Oldsmobile. That car belonged to Fred Landerson. He ran in the first automobile race that established the first speed record of 37 miles an hour. And that car came in fourth place behind Barney Oldfield, like I said, who had established the first speed record to 37 miles an hour. 37 miles an hour. I guess that's about all, isn't it? Let's see. You want to know something about me? That's what me that we came here in 1936. I went through the Coeur d'Alene school system, went through junior college. And the reason, when I got interested in the history, it happened to be a junior college assignment. I was asked to interview Mr. Johnson, who was a boat builder here in the early days. And he showed me pictures that he had of early day Coeur d'Alene and it intrigued me so much I decided I'd like to start Collecting a few of my own so I did And this is why I got into this thing and it's just pyramid When I put them up in the barber shop, the people kept bringing in other pictures. And before I realized that I had almost 300 of these antique pictures, my wife is beginning to call me an antique, too, because I'm getting too old for this thing. Thank you, Mr. Marvin Jones.
